item.text: The first experiment identifies the black single cell at `(18,27)` as the controllable avatar: actions 1–4 move it exactly three pixels up/down/left/right. Action 5 is contextual—it relocates the black cell to `(30,39)`—while two large colored structures also update every turn. The ten 3×3 rings strongly suggest level 1’s alignment mechanic in a larger configuration, so I’m testing the existing general leg on a level-2 clone before designing anything new.

The reuse failure is informative: level 2 replaces crosses with independently selectable diamond outlines. The ring centers are not merely axis markers; for color 13, all three lie exactly nine Manhattan pixels from `(12,21)`, matching that diamond’s radius. I’m computing shell centers symbolically and will then verify the inferred placements on clones against the actual reward.

The three movable objects are deliberately different geometries, so a single “diamond center” formula is too brittle. I’m switching to a more general observation: on a clone, move the selected object one lattice step, recover its translated pixel offsets from the delta, cycle selection with action 5, and search for translations whose outline covers every same-colored ring center. This directly tests each object’s affordance without assuming its shape.

The clone verification succeeded exactly: placing color 12 at `(48,18)` and color 13 at `(12,21)` gives dense partial progress but no reward; placing color 9 at `(48,27)` then raises `levels_completed` from 1 to 2. I’m now encoding that discovered operation as one reusable outline-to-ring leg and keeping `play_level_2` as a single leg composition.

Implemented and verified level 2.

- Added reusable `align_selected_outlines_to_ring_markers` leg in [legs.py](/Users/sasha/gkm/arc/crack_lab/runs/scratch/gkm_legs_ws_re86_cheap_medium_screen/legs.py).
- Added the level-2 composition in [players.py](/Users/sasha/gkm/arc/crack_lab/runs/scratch/gkm_legs_ws_re86_cheap_medium_screen/players.py).
- Removed the temporary probe script.
